coredata.dc:title: Tilted 3D evaporator with high-performance salt rejection for seawater desalination
coredata.dc:creator: Cuong, Nguyen Trong
Van Canh, Nguyen
Hoa, Nguyen Hiep
Pham, Thanh Hai
Pham, Hong Thach
Phong, Le Thi Hong
Tuyet, Cu Thi Anh
Hang, Nguyen Thi Nhat
Dao, Van-Duong
Nguyen, Vanthan
coredata.dc:description: Salt accumulation severely impacts the performance of solar desalination systems and hinders their long-term application in practice. Herein, we report a tilted 3D evaporator with high-performance salt-rejecting feature based on carbon nanodots (CDs)/luffa composite. The tilted 3D evaporator was designed by immersing one edge of CDs/luffa composite in a seawater tank and tilting at an angle to the horizontal plane. Upon solar irradiation, seawater was transferred through CDs/luffa composite at the bottom surface and wicked to the top surface for evaporation. Meanwhile, salt at the top surface was rejected back to the bottom surface and further taken away from the evaporator by the flowing seawater, thus avoiding salt accumulation for long-term solar desalination. Furthermore, the evaporator demonstrated effective thermal localization and absorption of environmental energy, possessing high evaporation rates of 2.05 and 1.50 kg m−2 h−1 when evaporation with water and real seawater, respectively (under one sun irradiation). Our study provides a promising strategy for salt rejection and enhancing the performance of solar desalination in practice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal materials
Water evaporation
Solar steam generation
Solar desalination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs/luffa composite
Solar steam generation and salt rejecting performance

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development

Trường Đại Học Thủ Dầu Một

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Vietnam Institute for Tropical Technology and Environmental Protection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Vietnam Institute for Tropical Technology and Environmental Protection (Grant No. 79-1/2023/HDKHCN ). This research is funded by Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 103.02-2021.106 . The authors acknowledge support and resources from Van Lang University , Ngo Quyen University , and Thu Dau Mot University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of solar steam generation and salt rejecting process based on tiled 3D evaporator.

Structures of CDs/luffa composite. (a, b) Photograph of treated luffa showing the 3D porous structure of luffa sponge. SEM images of (c) the luffa fiber and (d) cross-sectional and (e) side view showing the microchannel and micro-sized pores exist on the microchannel surface. (f, g) Photograph of CDs/luffa composite. (h–k) SEM images of CDs/luffa composite at different magnifications show that the 3D porous structure of luffa fiber with microchannel and micro-sized pores remained after being coated with CDs.

Properties of CDs/luffa composite. (a) Photographs of water swelling CDs/luffa composite after folding, pressing, and twisting. (b) FTIR spectra of treated luffa and CDs/luffa composite. (c) Absorption spectra of treated luffa and CDs/luffa composites. (d) IR images of CDs/luffa composite at initial and steady state after 5 min irradiation with solar power density of one sun. (e) Time-scale mass changes of water transfer through airlaid paper, treated luffa, and CDs/luffa composite at tilt angle of 30°. (f) Summary of the flow rates of CDs/luffa composite with high-salinity brine and real seawater.

Solar steam generation of the 3D evaporation. (a) Schematic illustrations of solar steam generation by horizontal 2D, horizontal 3D, and tilted 3D evaporators. (b) The mass changes of horizontal 2D, horizontal 3D, and 30° tilted 3D evaporators as a function of time under one sun irradiation. (c) The evaporation rates of 3D evaporator at different tilt angles under dark and one sun irradiation conditions. (d) IR images of 3D evaporation at initial and stable stages after 5 min irradiation and (e) corresponding temperature evolution of 3D evaporator at different testing positions under one sun irradiation.

Salt-rejecting performance of the tilted 3D evaporator. (a) The evaporation rates of tilted 3D evaporator for the evaporation of real seawater, 10 wt%, 15 wt%, and 20 wt% brine under one sun irradiation. (b) Images of tilted 3D evaporator at initial stage and after 8 h working with real seawater under one sun irradiation. (c) A progression of salt rejection from the horizontal 2D and tilted 3D evaporators, while under one sun irradiation. The evaporators were working with real seawater and 10 g of NaCl was placed on the surface of the evaporation structure. (d) Images of tilted 3D evaporator at initial stage and after 5 days working with real seawater under natural light.

Outdoor experiment of the solar still using tilted 3D evaporator on a partly sunny day with scattered clouds on a rooftop at Thu Dau Mot City (August 9, 2023). (a) Experimental setup. The experimental test consisted of (1) solar still, (2) real seawater tank (from Vung Tau Beach Vietnam), (3) Concentrated seawater tank, (4) Distilled water collection (b) Schematic of the experimental setup. The evaporator was tilted 30° with the horizontal direction. (c) Real-time variations of solar flux and ambient temperature during the outdoor test. (d) Ion concentrations in the distilled water and real seawater.

head.ce:title: Tilted 3D evaporator with high-performance salt rejection for seawater desalination

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: N. T. Cuong and N. V. Canh contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salt accumulation severely impacts the performance of solar desalination systems and hinders their long-term application in practice. Herein, we report a tilted 3D evaporator with high-performance salt-rejecting feature based on carbon nanodots (CDs)/luffa composite. The tilted 3D evaporator was designed by immersing one edge of CDs/luffa composite in a seawater tank and tilting at an angle to the horizontal plane. Upon solar irradiation, seawater was transferred through CDs/luffa composite at the bottom surface and wicked to the top surface for evaporation. Meanwhile, salt at the top surface was rejected back to the bottom surface and further taken away from the evaporator by the flowing seawater, thus avoiding salt accumulation for long-term solar desalination. Furthermore, the evaporator demonstrated effective thermal localization and absorption of environmental energy, possessing high evaporation rates of 2.05 and 1.50 kg m−2 h−1 when evaporation with water and real seawater, respectively (under one sun irradiation). Our study provides a promising strategy for salt rejection and enhancing the performance of solar desalination in practice.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar desalination is a promising technology to generate clean water from abundantly available seawater and sustainable solar energy in response to the most serious global challenges of water and energy scarcities [1,2]. Recently, emerging solar desalination by interfacial evaporation has attracted tremendous attention due to its high solar-to-vapor efficiency generated by effective solar absorber and improved thermal management [3–10]. Typically, solar heat converted by the solar absorber is localized into a small volume of water molecules on the evaporative surface, while bottom water is continuously pumped to the top surface by the capillary wicking effect of the porous evaporation structure [9,11,12]. By careful structural design of the evaporator, environmental energy can be effectively absorbed to enhance the performance of interfacial evaporation so that the water evaporation rate can be extremely higher than the theoretical limit of vapor output (about 1.47 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun (1 kW m−2) irradiation, assuming 100 % solar-to-vapor conversion efficiency) [13–19]. However, the fast water evaporation leads to salt accumulation on the evaporative surface and gradually blocks water paths for water supply, resulting in a significant decrease in evaporation rate [20–22]. Therefore, solar evaporators with high-performance salt rejection are highly desired for practical application of solar desalination.

Several strategies have been proposed to solve salt accumulation issues by using novel evaporator structures such as Janus film [23–27], introduction of millimeter-sized channel array [28], wick-free confined water layer [29], and fabric-based structure with flowing water [30–36]. In the first three strategies, the evaporator exhibited excellent salt-rejecting properties that can maintain a stable steam generation rate for long-term evaporation of high-salinity brine with over 80 % efficiency under one sun irradiation [28,29]. However, the water evaporation rates in these evaporators were typically less than the theoretical limit of vapor output due to the low environmental energy absorption of their flat 2D structures [25–29]. In the fabric-based evaporator with flowing water, the solar absorber layer was typically placed on a hydrophilic fabric layer [30,32,33]. The water transferred through the fabric layer can simultaneously supply water to the evaporative surface and take away high-salinity brine from the evaporator for salt rejection. The water evaporation rates of these fabric-based evaporators can be significantly improved by absorbing waste heat [32,37]. However, direct contact between the thin absorber layer and the flowing water layer in the fabric may reduce the thermal localization and lead to heat conduction loss to the flowing water, especially at high water flowing rate [30–32]. Furthermore, fabric-based evaporators suffer from limited energy absorption from the environment and inefficient vapor diffusion due to their flat structure, resulting in a low evaporation rate at low light intensity [19,36].

Here, we report a tilted 3D evaporator based on carbon nanodots (CDs)/luffa composite for effective salt rejection while simultaneously maintaining effective thermal localization and environmental energy absorption. One edge of CDs/luffa composite was immersed in a seawater tank and tilted at an angle to the horizontal plane (Fig. 1 ). Seawater was effectively transferred through CDs/luffa composite at the bottom surface and further wicked to the evaporative surface by capillary wicking of the microchannel in luffa fiber. During the evaporation, salt crystals were rejected from the top surface to the bottom surface through upward-wicking seawater in the microchannels. The salt crystals were effectively taken away from the evaporator by the flowing water at the bottom surface, thus avoiding salt accumulation for long-term solar desalination. Due to their 3D porous structure, the evaporator exhibited good thermal localization that heat was mainly localized on the top surface. Furthermore, flowing water in the 3D porous structure can effectively absorb environmental energy and the evaporation rates reached 2.05 and 1.50 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation when evaporation with water and real seawater, respectively.

Luffa was treated by cooking the luffa sponge in a mixture solution (500 mL) of sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 60 g) and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, 9 g) at 100 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the luffa sponge was washed with deionized water and further cooked in 10 % H2O2 solution at 100 °C for 30 min to completely remove the residual lignin.

CDs were synthesized by solvothermal treatment of citric acid (6 g) and urea (12 g) in dimethyl sulfoxide solution (DMSO, 40 mL) at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged and further purified via dialysis to remove unreacted precursors and byproducts. Finally, the purified solution was dried at 80 °C to get black CDs powder.

To prepare CDs/luffa and CDs/paper composites, treated luffa (4 cm × 12 cm) and airlaid paper sheet (4 cm × 12 cm, 90 gsm) were immersed and treated with 125 mL CDs solution (80 mg/mL) in a 250 mL Teflon autoclave at 140 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the obtained composites were washed with water and dried at 80 °C for further characterization.

A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) was used to characterize the structures of treated luffa and CDs/luffa composite. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs were obtained via a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM 2100). A Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer was used to record FTIR spectra. Absorption and reflectance measurements were performed via UH4150 spectrophotometer (Hitachi). Infrared (IR) images were taken using FLIR TG267 thermal camera. The mechanical properties of materials were tested by YM-H42 universal material testing machine (Yang Yi).

In the tilted 3D evaporator, the CDs/luffa composite with size of 4 cm × 12 cm was fixed on the inner walls of two polystyrene-foam water tanks (inner size: 4 cm × 5 cm × 6 cm). Two water tanks were placed at different heights and the upper tank was filled with water or seawater while the lower tank was left empty for collecting the transfer water. The higher edge of CDs/luffa composite was immersed in the upper water tank. The top surfaces of the two water tanks were covered by floating polystyrene foams to reduce the natural evaporation of the bulk water. The horizontal distance of the two water tanks remained unchanged at 4 cm and the height difference of the two water tanks was changed to form different tilt angles (θ) between the CDs/luffa composite and the horizontal plane.

To test the solar steam generation of the tilted 3D evaporator, the CDs/luffa composite between two water tanks was irradiated by Aulight CEL-HXF300-T3 solar simulator with irradiation size of 4 cm × 4 cm. The solar power densities were determined by TM-750 solar power meter (Tenmars). The temperatures of the evaporator were measured using a thermal camera (FLIR TG267) and K-type thermocouples (Tenmars, TM-321N). The mass changes of water were recorded in real time by an analytical balance (Vibra AF-220E, Shinko Denshi) during the evaporation. The mass changes of the system were further corrected by subtracting the mass changes by natural evaporation of bulk water in two water tanks (without CDs/luffa composite). For comparison, a horizontal 3D evaporator was also set up by placing two water tanks at the same height. In a horizontal 2D evaporator, CDs/paper composite with size of 4 cm × 12 cm was affixed on the top surface of a plastic frame (top surface size of 4 cm × 4 cm). Two edges of CDs/paper composite were immersed in bulk water and the height from the surface of the bulk water to the top surface was 2 cm.

For the outdoor test of solar still, a large tilted 3D evaporator (size: 5 cm × 12 cm) composed of 3 discrete CDs/luffa composites was placed on two water tanks with a tilt angle of 30°. All the evaporator was placed in a condensation structure made of mica glass and polystyrene foam (size: 25 cm × 30 cm × 15 cm). Real seawater from Vung Tau Beach (Vietnam) with salinity of 3.1 % was used for the desalination. The outdoor test was conducted on a partly sunny day with scattered clouds on a rooftop at Thu Dau Mot City (August 9, 2023). The corresponding solar intensity and ambient temperature were recorded every 15 min. The concentrations of the ions in seawater and distilled water were measured by an ion chromatography system with Waters 432 conductivity detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs/luffa composite
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/luffa composite was fabricated by coating CDs on the 3D porous structure of luffa sponge. The natural luffa was chemically treated to remove lignin before CDs coating. The shape of the luffa was changed from a cylindrical shape (6 cm diameter) to a ribbon-like shape with a height of about 0.5 cm after the treatment (Fig. 2a). The digital image of the treated luffa sponge shows that the 3D porous structure of the luffa sponge remained after the chemical treatment (Fig. 2b). Different luffa fibers were stacked together to form 3D porous structure with different porosity. The morphology and structure of the luffa fiber were further characterized by SEM (Fig. 2c-e). The cross-sectional and side-view SEM images of treated luffa show microchannel and micro-sized pores structures in the luffa fibers. CDs solar absorber with broad light absorption features was synthesized by solvothermal treatment of citric acid and urea in dimethyl sulfoxide solution (Fig. S1, Supplementary material) [38]. The treated luffa was heated with high-concentration CDs solution in a Teflon autoclave at 140 °C to form CDs/luffa composite. After the reaction, the CDs/luffa composite's color turned from bright brown to black (Fig. 2f). As shown in digital image (Fig. 2g) and SEM images (Fig. 2h - k), the 3D porous structure as well as the microchannel and micro-sized pores in luffa fiber remained unchanged after coating with CDs. These results indicate that CDs with small size can be coated uniformly on the luffa fiber surfaces without clogging the microchannel and micro-sized pores of luffa fiber. The 3D porous features of CDs/luffa composite enabled effective light absorption and diffusion of vapor into the atmospheric environment during the evaporation. Meanwhile, the microchannels transported water through the luffa fiber by capillary wicking effect while the micro-sized pores connected these microchannels and delivered water to the outer surface of the luffa fiber [39,40].

The CDs/luffa composite possessed good mechanical properties for long-term use in solar steam generation. Compared with raw luffa, the CDs/luffa composite demonstrated a significant improvement of elongation at break (increasing by 253.5 %) while still maintaining good tensile strength, as shown in strain-stress curves (Fig. S2). The CDs/luffa composite can be repeatedly folded, pressed, and twisted without breaking and maintains its original shape very well (Fig. 3a). In addition, the CDs strongly adhered on luffa fiber and the black color of CDs/luffa composite was not obviously changed after repeatedly washed with water. The strong interaction between CDs and luffa fiber may be attributed to the hydrogen bonding as well as chemical bonding between functional groups on CDs and cellulose in the luffa fiber. FTIR measurement further confirmed the presence of CDs on the surfaces of luffa fibers and enriched hydrophilic functional groups of the CDs/luffa composite. As shown in Fig. 3b, the FTIR spectrum of CDs/luffa composite showed broader and more intense absorption bands of hydrophilic functional groups compared with that of treated luffa. For example, there were intense absorption bands of OH and NH bonds at 3100–3700 cm−1, CO bond at 1650 cm−1, and CO bond at 1040 cm−1. Most of the absorption-band features of the CDs/luffa composite were similar to those of CDs (Fig. S3) [41,42] indicating abundant functional groups of the CDs/luffa composite mainly originated from CDs. These hydrophilic functional groups enable a rapid and continuous supply of water by capillary force through microchannels of luffa fibers for solar steam generation.

We further evaluate the absorption capacity and photothermal properties of the CDs/luffa composite. As shown in the absorption spectra (Figs. 3c, S4), the CDs/luffa composite showed good light absorption features in a broad range of 200–2400 nm with an average absorption of 80 % in the range of 200–1400 nm. The enhancement of the absorption capacity of CDs/luffa composite originated from the effective light absorption of CDs in the visible and NIR regions. As shown in the transmittance spectra (Fig. S4), there is nearly no light transmitted through the CDs/luffa composite, suggesting that the influence of the porous structure of luffa on solar absorption can be ignored. To investigate the photothermal properties, the CDs/luffa composite was irradiated with a solar simulator at power density of one sun, and their surface temperatures were monitored by a thermal camera. The surface temperature of the CDs/luffa composite increased quickly from room temperature of 27.0 °C to reached stable temperature of 42.2 °C after 5 min irradiation (Fig. 3d). The results indicate that the CDs/luffa composite can effectively capture solar energy and produce high photothermal conversion.

Due to the existence of microchannels and abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of luffa fibers, the CDs/luffa composite exhibited excellent water transfer capacity. The details of the experimental setup for water transfer test are shown in Fig. S5. At the same 30o tilted angle between the CDs/luffa composite and horizontal plane, the water flow rate of CDs/luffa composite was 7.2 g min−1, which was about 1.8 times and 10 times higher than those of treated luffa and general airlaid paper, respectively (Fig. 3e). This result indicates that coating with CDs significantly improved the hydrophilic property of CDs/luffa composite, subsequently enhancing the water transfer capacity. The flow rate of water was also affected by the tilt angle of the CDs/luffa composite and the flow rate increased with the increase of tilt angle (Fig. S6). Importantly, the CDs/luffa composite maintained high water-transfer capacity when working with high-salinity brine and real seawater (Figs. 3f, S7). The water flow rate remained at a high value of around 2.4 g min−1 for 20 wt% salinity brine and real seawater (Vung Tau Beach), which was still 3.3 times higher than that of pure water transferring through airlaid paper. Since the complex mixture of the real seawater, the flow rate of real seawater is low. Therefore, the high flow rate of the CDs/luffa composite when working with real seawater is essential for seawater evaporation. To further explore the water-transfer process in CDs/luffa composite, an experiment of wet treated-luffa transferring methylene blue solution was carried out. The transferring progression of methylene blue solution showed that methylene blue was mainly transferred at the bottom surface of the luffa (Fig. S8). This can be explained by the effect of gravity force. Therefore, water was transferred through CDs/luffa composite at the bottom surface from the higher water tank to the lower tank because of the cooperation of capillary force, gravity, and siphon effect [32,43]. Furthermore, the water can be wicked to the top surface from the bottom surface by the capillary wicking effect of the microchannel of luffa fiber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar steam generation and salt rejecting performance
ce:section.ce:para: A tilted 3D evaporator was developed by fixing the CDs/luffa composite on the inner walls of two water tanks. The upper tank was filled with water and the water was continuously transferred through the CDs/luffa composite and reached the lower tank (Fig. 4a). At tilt angle of 30°, the tilted 3D evaporator exhibited a high evaporation rate of 2.05 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation (Fig. 4b). This evaporation rate of the tilted 3D evaporator was 2.5 times higher than that of a horizontal 2D evaporator made of CDs/paper composite (0.83 kg m−2 h−1), and much higher than that of other conventional luffa-based evaporators [40,44–49]. When two water tanks were placed at the same height, the tilted 3D evaporator became a horizontal 3D evaporator and the evaporation rate decreased to a lower value of 1.29 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation. This result indicates that the steam generation performance of the tilted 3D evaporator was strongly affected by the tilt angle. As shown in Figs. 4c and S9, the evaporation rates under both one sun irradiation and dark condition were increased with increasing the tilt angle and reached a maximum value at tilt angle of 30°. Further increasing the tilt angle to 45°, the evaporation rate decreased, which can be attributed to the heat loss to the high-flowing water at the bottom surface. The evaporation rate under dark condition was significantly increased from 0.63 kg m−2 h−1 to a high value of 1.61 kg m−2 h−1 when the tilt angle changed from 0° to 30°. At the same tilt angle, the different values between the evaporation rates under one sun irradiation and dark condition were not significantly changed. Thus, the increase in the evaporation rate of the tilted 3D evaporator originated from the increase of dark evaporation by absorbing environmental energy. These results indicate that the flowing water in the 3D porous structure can effectively contacted with ambient environment, subsequently, enhancing energy gain from the environment.

The temperature measurement of the tilted 3D evaporator demonstrates simultaneous thermal localization and environmental energy absorption during the evaporation. The surface temperature of the tilted 3D evaporator increased quickly and reached stable after 5 min under one sun irradiation (Fig. 4d). To record the real-time temperature evolution of the evaporator during the irradiation, three K-type thermocouples were inserted into the evaporator at different positions of the top surface, middle, and bottom surface. The temperature of the top surface increased from initial 23.5 °C to 32.2 °C within 150 s and reached a stable temperature of 33.0 °C under one sun irradiation (Fig. 4e). In contrast, the stable temperatures at the middle and bottom surfaces of the evaporator were much lower than that of the top surface. Clearly, the evaporator exhibited good thermal localization despite the presence of flowing water at its bottom surface. Remarkably, the temperature of the bottom surface only slightly increased and was still lower than the environmental temperature (27.5 °C). Thus, the flowing water at the bottom surface can absorb energy from the environment to improve the evaporation rate. Noteworthily, at dark condition, the surface temperatures of the evaporator are low and more energy can be gained from the environment and a high dark evaporation rate can be obtained. In contrast, the temperature of the evaporator increases under sunlight irradiation, which decreases energy gain from the environment by the evaporator [18]. As a result, the contribution of energy gain from the environment for water evaporation under sunlight irradiation is lower than that under dark condition. The effective absorption of energy from the environment is important to maintain high performance of solar desalination in practical applications with the intensity fluctuation of natural sunlight.

We further demonstrate that the high evaporation rate can be maintained for evaporation with seawater. Real seawater from Vung Tau Beach and a series of high-salinity brine (10 wt%, 15 wt%, and 20 wt%) were used to test with the tilted 3D evaporator. Compared with NaCl simulated seawater, evaporating real seawater is more challenging because the presence of biofouling and crystallization of the minor species can clog the porous structure, resulting in a reduction in evaporation rate [31,43]. In this work, the tilted 3D evaporator still possessed a high evaporation rate of 1.50 kg m−2 h−1 when working with real seawater under one sun irradiation (Fig. 5a). For high-salinity brine, the evaporation rate was 1.56 kg m−2 h−1 at 10 wt% salinity and gradually decreased with the increase of salinity due to the reduced water activity of salt solutions [29].

The salt-rejecting capability of the tilted 3D evaporator was carefully characterized. No salt crystallization was observed at the top surface of the evaporator after 8 h continuous testing with real seawater under one sun irradiation (Fig. 5b). A stable evaporation rate was also achieved during the 8 h continuous test (Fig. S10) indicating the high stability of the evaporator for long-term solar desalination. We further examined the salt rejection under steady-state evaporation condition under one sun irradiation. For comparison, the salt rejection experiment of a conventional horizontal 2D evaporator of CDs/paper composite was also carried out. The horizontal 2D and tilted 3D evaporators were working with real seawater, and 10 g of additional solid NaCl were placed directly on their evaporative surface. As shown in Fig. 5c, the 2D horizontal evaporator clearly illustrated a weak salt rejecting capacity of the conventional evaporator that most of the solid salt remained at the evaporative surface after a long-time test. In contrast, the tilted 3D evaporator completely rejected the salt within 60 min while generating vapor under one sun irradiation, indicating the excellent salt-rejecting properties of the tilted 3D evaporator.

We further examine the performance of the tilted 3D evaporator with real seawater under natural light. The evaporator was tested on a partly sunny day with scattered clouds from 10:00 to 16:00 (December 31, 2023) on a rooftop at Thu Dau Mot City (Binh Duong, Vietnam). The evaporation rate fluctuated from 3.25 to 5.33 kg m−2 h−1 (Fig. S11), demonstrating its high evaporative performance. Clearly, the evaporation rate in the outdoor experiment was higher than that in the indoor experiment. This result indicates that more energy gain from the environment was achieved due to higher ambient temperature and higher wind speed in the outdoor experiment. In addition, vapor can effectively diffuse to the ambient in the outdoor experiment, subsequently, improving the evaporation rate. For salt rejection in the outdoor experiment, the tilted 3D evaporator worked with real seawater under natural light for 5 days (Figs. S12 and S13). The digital images of the tilted 3D evaporator during the experiment show that no salt crystallization was observed at the top surface of the evaporator (Fig. 5d). These results indicate that the tilted 3D evaporator possesses excellent salt-rejecting properties for long-term seawater desalination.

The high-performance salt rejection of the tilted 3D evaporator can be attributed to the cooperation of effective water wicking and water transferring in the evaporator. Firstly, because of the fast evaporation of water by sunlight irradiation, the salt concentration of seawater at the top surface of the evaporator was much higher than that at the bottom surface. As a result, salt crystals rejected from the top surface to the bottom surface while the seawater wicked upward through the microchannels of luffa fibers. Secondly, these salt crystals were taken away from the evaporator by the flowing water at the bottom surface. Due to the continuous flow of water from raw seawater, the seawater at the bottom surface remained low salinity. Thus, this ensured the salt concentration gradient between the top surface and the bottom surface, generating a high-performance salt rejection for continuous solar desalination. The salt-rejecting performance of the tilted 3D evaporator was superior to that of other 3D evaporators such as cylinder [50,51], heat sink [14], and tree-inspired structures [13], in which the salt accumulation may be more serious when increasing the height of evaporator to enhance energy gain from environment. Our results demonstrated the simultaneous salt rejection, thermal localization, and environmental energy absorption during solar evaporation of the tilted 3D evaporator.

To demonstrate the potential and advantages for practical desalination application, we performed outdoor seawater desalination of solar still using a 30° tilted 3D evaporator. The tilted 3D evaporator with size of 5 cm × 12 cm was placed in a condensation structure made of low-cost materials, i.e., mica glass and polystyrene foam (Fig. 6a, b). Real seawater was transferred to the upper seawater tank from a big seawater tank placed outside the solar still. Meanwhile, the concentrated seawater in the lower seawater tank was transferred out to ensure the continuous working of this solar still. All the system was placed on a rooftop at Thu Dau Mot City. The experiment was carried out on a partly sunny day with scattered clouds (August 9, 2023) from 8:30 am to 4:0 pm. The solar flux and ambient temperature during the outdoor desalination test were recorded and shown in Fig. 6c. During the outdoor test, 65.1 g of clean water was collected, corresponding to a production rate of 10.85 kg m−2 per day, which is enough to meet the minimum drinking water demands for three adults per day. Furthermore, the major ion (i.e., Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) concentrations of the collected water were all lower than 10 mg L−1 (Fig. 6d) after the desalination, meeting the World Health Organization (WHO) standard for drinking water. These results reveal that the tilted 3D evaporator shows great potential for practical solar desalination. In addition, because of their ribbon-like shape and good mechanical properties, the tilted 3D evaporator can be applied to the multistage solar desalination system to improve water productivity [52].

In summary, we demonstrated a tilted 3D evaporator based on CDs/luffa composite with high-performance salt rejection for seawater desalination. The tilted 3D evaporator was designed by immersing one edge of CDs/luffa composite in a seawater tank and tilting at an angle to the horizontal plane. Seawater can effectively transfer through CDs/luffa composite at the bottom surface and further wicked to evaporative surface via microchannel in luffa fiber by the cooperation of capillary wicking, gravity, and siphon effect. Upon solar irradiation, salt crystals were rejected from top surface to bottom surface through upward wicking seawater, subsequently, effectively taken away from the evaporator by the flowing seawater. Because of their 3D porous structure, the evaporator demonstrated good thermal localization on the top surface while the temperature of the bottom surface remained lower than that of the environment. As a result, flowing water at the bottom surface can effectively absorb environmental energy and significantly enhance the evaporation rates that its values reached 2.05 and 1.50 kg m−2 h−1 when evaporation with water and real seawater, respectively (under one sun irradiation). The structure of a tilted 3D evaporator provides a promising strategy to improve the salt rejection of solar desalination systems while still maintaining their important features of effective thermal localization and environmental energy absorption.

Nguyen Trong Cuong: Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Nguyen Van Canh: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Nguyen Hiep Hoa: Investigation. Thanh Hai Pham: Resources. Hong Thach Pham: Resources. Le Thi Hong Phong: Resources. Cu Thi Anh Tuyet: Visualization. Nguyen Thi Nhat Hang: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Van-Duong Dao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Vanthan Nguyen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Vietnam Institute for Tropical Technology and Environmental Protection (Grant No. 79-1/2023/HDKHCN). This research is funded by Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 103.02-2021.106. The authors acknowledge support and resources from Van Lang University, Ngo Quyen University, and Thu Dau Mot University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2024.117303.
